key_insight: Conditioning synaptic noise with a Doob h-transform can turn intrinsic analog device noise from a liability into a consolidation mechanism that improves continual-learning retention, with an inverted-U dependence on noise.
engineering_relevance: For analog neuromorphic hardware, the result suggests that carefully controlled intrinsic noise can be exploited to stabilize older memories instead of being minimized, potentially improving stability-plasticity tradeoffs.
method: Model each synapse as a stochastic diffusion conditioned to never cross a memory-critical barrier around its consolidated weight.
Add the Doob-induced restoring drift term sigma^2 d/dw log h on top of an anchored drift baseline from established consolidation methods.
Test the rule on Split-MNIST and on BrainScaleS-2 silicon with intrinsic noise included in the training loop.
evidence_signal: On single-head Split-MNIST, the rule improved retention by 10.9 points at an interior noise optimum, and on BrainScaleS-2 it retained a prior task 15.6 points better than the matched control at matched average accuracy.
watch_item: The hardware result is reported from a single seed and the benefit is a retention gain rather than a net-accuracy improvement, so broader replication and deployment calibration are needed.
paper_title: Intrinsic-Noise Consolidation: A Doob-Barrier-Conditioned Diffusion Turns Analog Device Noise into a Continual-Learning Resource
